coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as eco-friendly fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective determination of lumateperone in pharmaceutical preparation: Greenness and blueness assessment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Arwa Sultan
Almutairi, Farooq M.
Aldhafeeri, Muneef M.
Althobaiti, Yusuf S.
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Serag, Ahmed
Almalki, Atiah H.
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the potential of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probes for the determination of the antipsychotic drug lumateperone. The spectral characteristics and sensing mechanism of the fluorescent probes were examined, revealing a static quenching mechanism as indicated by the Stern-Volmer analysis. Different factors affecting the quenching process, such as pH, concentration of QDs, and incubation time, were carefully tuned. The developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines, demonstrating excellent linearity in the range of 0.5–2.5 μg/mL, limits of detection and quantification of 0.1226 μg/mL and 0.3714 μg/mL, respectively, and high accuracy, precision, robustness and selectivity. Furthermore, the greenness and blueness of the proposed method were assessed using GAPI, AGREE, and BAGI tools, yielding an AGREE score of 0.78 and a BAGI score of 75 confirming its environmentally benign nature as well as analytical practicality. The method was successfully applied to determine lumateperone in a pharmaceutical formulation, highlighting its potential for routine quality control analysis. This study demonstrates the promising analytical capabilities of GQDs for the sensitive and selective detection of antipsychotic drug lumateperone, offering a simple, rapid, and green alternative to existing analytical techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Lumateperone
Fluorescent probes
AGREE
BAGI

The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of the lumateperone functionalized GQDs showing a potent emission at a λmax of 447 nm when excited at 355 nm. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra resulting from the quenching process of the functionalized GQDs with various lumateperone concentrations.
Stern–Volmer chart demonstrating the interaction between lumateperone and the developed GQDs at different temperatures.

Investigation of experimental factors impacting the stability of fluorescence quenching of GQDs by lumateperone, such as (A) pH of the buffer, (B) quantity of the GQDs, and (C) duration of incubation. The error bars illustrate the standard error derived from three independent measurements.
The competitive selectivity experiments showing the fluorescence quenching factor [(F0 − F)/F0] responses of the developed GQDs interaction with lumateperone and various interfering compounds.

Greenness assessment of the developed method using the (A) GAPI and (B) AGREE tools to determine their environmental impact. (C) Blueness assessment of the developed method using the BAGI tool to determine its analytical practicability.
Validation data for determination of lumateperone by the developed functionalized GQDs fluorescent probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Selectivity study of the proposed method using standard addition technique.
Quantitative analysis of lumateperone in the commercial Caplyta® capsules by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P = 0.05).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as eco-friendly fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective determination of lumateperone in pharmaceutical preparation: Greenness and blueness assessment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the potential of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probes for the determination of the antipsychotic drug lumateperone. The spectral characteristics and sensing mechanism of the fluorescent probes were examined, revealing a static quenching mechanism as indicated by the Stern-Volmer analysis. Different factors affecting the quenching process, such as pH, concentration of QDs, and incubation time, were carefully tuned. The developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines, demonstrating excellent linearity in the range of 0.5–2.5 μg/mL, limits of detection and quantification of 0.1226 μg/mL and 0.3714 μg/mL, respectively, and high accuracy, precision, robustness and selectivity. Furthermore, the greenness and blueness of the proposed method were assessed using GAPI, AGREE, and BAGI tools, yielding an AGREE score of 0.78 and a BAGI score of 75 confirming its environmentally benign nature as well as analytical practicality. The method was successfully applied to determine lumateperone in a pharmaceutical formulation, highlighting its potential for routine quality control analysis. This study demonstrates the promising analytical capabilities of GQDs for the sensitive and selective detection of antipsychotic drug lumateperone, offering a simple, rapid, and green alternative to existing analytical techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lumateperone, a novel antipsychotic agent, has emerged as a promising treatment for individuals suffering from schizophrenia and bipolar depression [1–3]. Approved by the FDA in 2019 for schizophrenia and in 2021 for bipolar depression, lumateperone exerts its therapeutic effects through a unique mechanism of action [2]. It acts as a serotonin 5-HT2A receptor antagonist, dopamine D2 receptor presynaptic partial agonist and postsynaptic antagonist, and glutamatergic phosphoprotein modulator [4]. This distinctive pharmacological profile allows lumateperone to modulate multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously, potentially offering a more comprehensive approach to symptom management. The drug's partial agonist activity at dopamine D2 receptors may contribute to normalizing dopamine transmission while reducing the risk of excessive dopamine blockade-related side effects [5]. Clinical trials have demonstrated its efficacy in reducing both positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia, with a notable improvement in social functioning and cognitive performance [6]. In bipolar depression, lumateperone has shown significant improvements in depressive symptoms [7]. Moreover, lumateperone's favorable metabolic profile, with minimal impact on weight, lipids, and glucose levels, sets it apart from other second-generation antipsychotics [8]. Its lower risk of extrapyramidal symptoms and metabolic disturbances makes it an attractive option for long-term treatment, potentially improving patient adherence and quality of life [8].

Due to its recent approval, sensitive and reliable analytical methods are needed to monitor lumateperone levels in biological samples and pharmaceutical formulations. However, the literature revealed very few analytical methods for lumateperone quantification. LC-MS/MS technique has been reported for the determination of lumateperone in rat plasma with evaluation of its pharmacokinetics [9]. The study identified 14 phase I metabolites in liver microsomes and 10 in rat plasma, elucidating the main metabolic pathways of lumateperone. While the developed LC-MS/MS method was successfully validated and applied to pharmacokinetic studies in rats, it has limitations for routine pharmaceutical analysis. These include the requirement for complex instrumentation, a focus on metabolites rather than direct drug quantification in formulations, and the absence of greenness assessments. Another study has reported the use of HPLC-UV for the determination of lumateperone in pharmaceutical formulations, but this method also used complex chromatography conditions and lacked comprehensive greenness evaluation [10]. In addition, the use of methanol as an organic solvent raises concerns about the environmental impact and toxicity.

Spectrofluorometric methods based on fluorescent probes offer an attractive alternative for the determination of pharmaceutical compounds due to their simplicity, sensitivity, and eco-friendly nature [11–13]. The simplicity and ease-of-use of fluorescence-based analytical techniques make them particularly suitable for routine quality control analysis in pharmaceutical laboratories particularly in resource limited settings [14,15]. Among the different fluorescent probes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered significant interest due to their unique optical properties, biocompatibility, and tunable fluorescence [16]. These dots have been extensively explored for the sensitive and selective detection of various analytes, including pharmaceuticals, in complex matrices [17]. In addition, GQDs offer advantages such as facile synthesis, high photostability, and the ability to provide information on the sensing mechanism through fluorescence quenching studies [18]. Regarding their greenness properties, the use of graphene quantum dots as analytical probes is in line with the principles of green chemistry, as they are typically synthesized from renewable and sustainable precursors, and the detection process is often solvent-free and generates minimal waste [19].

Hence, the aim of the present research is to develop a simple, sensitive, and eco-friendly spectrofluorometric method for the determination of the antipsychotic drug lumateperone using GQDs as fluorescent probes. The spectral characteristics and sensing mechanism of fluorescent probes were investigated. Different factors affecting the quenching process, such as pH, concentration of QDs, and incubation time, were optimized to achieve maximum sensitivity. The method was then validated according to ICH guidelines to ensure its reliability and robustness. Additionally, the greenness and blueness assessment of the developed method was performed using the GAPI [20], AGREE [21], and BAGI [22] tools to evaluate its environmental impact and analytical practicality. Finally, the validated method was successfully applied to determine lumateperone in pharmaceutical formulations, demonstrating its potential for routine quality control analysis for this novel drug.

Lumateperone tosylate with certified purity (99.80 %) and GQDs were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The commercial lumateperone-containing pharmaceutical formulation (Caplyta®) was manufactured by Intra-Cellular claimed to contain 42 mg lumateperone per capsule. All supplementary solvents and reagents employed in the experiment were of high analytical grade and procured from El Nasr Company. Freshly distilled water was utilized during the process. Buffer solutions with varying pH values were prepared in accordance with the recommendations outlined in the United States Pharmacopoeia.

Functionalized GQDs were fabricated through a straightforward reaction [23]. 100 mL of 10−3 M tetraphenyl borate was combined with 100 mL of 10−3 M lumateperone prepared in a hydrochloric acid buffer at pH 2.0 to obtain an ion associate precipitate. The precipitate was kept in its mother liquor for 24 h, filtered, washed with water, and dried. One hundred mg of the dried precipitate was then mixed with 100 mg of GQDs, and the mixture was dissolved in 80 mL of distilled water. The suspension was treated by sonication for 2 h, and the volume was adjusted to 100 mL to yield a 2 mg/mL lumateperone-GQD solution.

Standard stock solutions of lumateperone were prepared by dissolving 10 mg of the drug in 100 mL of hydrochloric acid buffer with pH 2.0, yielding a concentration of 100 μg/mL. A working solution was then obtained by further diluting the stock solution with the same buffer to reach a final concentration of 20 μg/mL.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer. The settings were as follows: Excitation wavelength – 355 nm, Emission wavelength – 400–500 nm, Excitation slit width – 5 nm, Emission slit width – 5 nm, and Sensitivity – medium. All fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature in a 1 cm quartz cell. Spectra Manager II software was used for data acquisition and processing. A Jenway 3510 pH meter was utilized for pH measurements. Graph Pad Prism was employed for statistical analysis and plotting the graphs.

Different volumes of the standard lumateperone working solution were added to a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, resulting in final concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 μg/mL. To each flask, 2 mL of the graphene quantum dot solution (2 mg/mL) was added, and the mixtures were diluted to the mark with an acid phthalate buffer at pH 3. The solutions were then left to stand for 4 min before measuring their fluorescence intensity at an emission wavelength of 447 nm and an excitation wavelength of 355 nm (F). A similar procedure was performed for a blank solution containing only the GQD solution and buffer and its intensity was used as reference (F0). The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the net fluorescence intensity (F0/F) against the lumateperone concentration, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the blank and sample solutions, respectively, the regression equation and correlation coefficient were determined.

The developed spectrofluorometric method was validated according to the ICH guidelines [24] for the following parameters: linearity, accuracy, precision, limit of detection, and limit of quantitation, robustness and selectivity. The linearity of the method was evaluated by preparing a series of lumateperone standard solutions at concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 μg/mL. The accuracy of the method was determined by recovery studies at three different concentration levels 1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 μg/mL. Intra-day and inter-day precisions were evaluated by analyzing three different concentrations of lumateperone (1.0, 1.5 and 2.0 μg/m) on the same day and on three consecutive days, respectively. The detection limit and quantitation limit were calculated based on the residual standard deviation of the response and the slope of the calibration curve.

Robustness of the method was assessed by deliberately varying key parameters such as pH of the buffer solution (3.0 ± 0.1), incubation time (4 ± 0.5 min), and concentration of GQDs (2 ± 0.1 mL). Selectivity was determined by analyzing the response of the functionalized GQDs to different potential interfering substances that could be present in the pharmaceutical formulation as well as different metals and anions. In addition, standard addition technique was performed to verify the absence of matrix effects at three concentration levels of lumateperone (0.5, 0.75 and 1.0 μg/mL) spiked to the commercial capsule preparation. Recovery and percent relative standard deviation values were calculated to evaluate the selectivity.

The proposed fluorometric method was applied to the determination of lumateperone in the commercial Caplyta® capsule. Five capsules were evacuated, weighed, and mixed. An equivalent of 10 mg of lumateperone was accurately weighed and dissolved in 100 mL hydrochloric acid buffer with pH 2.0. The solution was sonicated for 20 min, filtered and appropriately diluted with the buffer solution to obtain a final concentration of 20 μg/mL. The sample was analyzed following the same procedure previously described for the calibration curve and the content of lumateperone was calculated using the regression equation.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the functionalized GQDs showed a strong peak centered at 447 nm upon excitation at 355 nm (Fig. 1 A). The observed fluorescence was attributed to the unique optical properties of the GQDs, which originate from electronic transitions such as π–π* transitions in the sp2 hybridized carbon network in addition to n–π* transitions of carbonyl groups on the QD surface [25]. Upon addition of lumateperone, a significant quenching of the fluorescence intensity was observed, which increased upon increasing the drug concentration (Fig. 1B).

The decrease in the fluorescence intensity can be attributed to either a static quenching mechanism, where the drug molecules form non-fluorescent complexes with the GQDs, or a dynamic quenching mechanism, where the drug molecules interact with the GQDs in the excited state and facilitate non-radiative transitions. Besides, the fluorescence quenching can also be due to the inner filter effect, where the drug absorbs the excitation light and/or emitted fluorescence, thereby reducing the number of photons reaching the detector. Hence, a temperature-dependent fluorescence study was carried out which revealed a decrease in the Stern-Volmer quenching constant with increasing temperature, indicating a static quenching mechanism prevailing in the system (Fig. 2 ). The Ksv value decreased from 6.95 × 105 M−1 at 25 °C to 5.78 × 105 M−1 at 30 °C and then to 5.15 × 105 M−1 at 40 °C confirming the static nature of the quenching process. This static quenching could be attributed to the formation of a nonfluorescent complex between the GQDs and lumateperone through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions, thus affecting the electronic transitions of the GQDs and consequently reducing the fluorescence intensity. It should be noted that the influence of inner filter effects has been ruled out, as lumateperone did not exhibit any considerable absorption within the excitation and emission wavelength regions of the graphene quantum dot probe. This supports the conclusion that static quenching is the predominant mechanism underlying the observed fluorescence quenching.

The effect of different experimental parameters such as pH, concentration of GQDs and incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by lumateperone was investigated to obtain the optimal conditions for the proposed method (Fig. 3 ). The aim was to achieve maximum sensitivity of the fluorometric detection. Different pH values of the buffer solutions were tested in the range of 2.0–10.0 (Fig. 3A). The maximum fluorescence quenching was observed at pH 3.0, which was selected as the optimum pH for further experiments (Fig. 3A). Although these results contra-indicate previous literature where GQDs exhibit enhanced fluorescence under basic pH due to increased emissive traps, the observed quenching at acidic pH in the present study could be attributed to the protonation of amine groups in lumateperone molecule, which facilitates its interaction with the GQDs surface and also increases the solubility of the drug. It is worth mentioning that lowering pH values beyond 3.0 resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs, this could be due to the decomposition of the GQDs under highly acidic conditions. So, a compromise between maximum drug-GQD interaction and maintaining the stability of the GQDs was achieved at pH 3.0. The influence of the concentration of GQDs on the fluorescence quenching was evaluated in the range of 0.5–5.0 mL of 2 mg/mL GQDs solution. The maximum quenching was observed with 2.0 mL of the GQDs solution, which provided sufficient surface area for interaction with lumateperone (Fig. 3B). In addition, the effect of incubation time was studied from 1 to 10 min, and maximum quenching was observed after 4 min of incubation, which was selected as the optimum time for the analysis (Fig. 3C).

The analytical validation parameters were evaluated according to the ICH guidelines (Table 1 ). Linearity was achieved in the concentration range of 0.5–2.5 μg/mL of lumateperone with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9998. The LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.1226 μg/mL and 0.3714 μg/mL, respectively, transcending the reported UV spectrophotometric and chromatographic methods [10], posing sufficient sensitivity for the determination of the drug in pharmaceutical preparations (Table 1). The method was evaluated for precision by calculating the intra-day and inter-day % RSD values, which were less than 1.5 % for both repeatability and intermediate precision, indicating good method precision (Table 1). The accuracy of the method was assessed by recovery studies, which gave an average recovery of 100.47 ± 0.685 %, demonstrating the reliability of the method for the quantification of lumateperone (Table 1). Robustness was evaluated by introducing deliberate changes in the experimental parameters like pH, GQDs concentration and incubation time, and the% RSD values were found to be less than 1 % for all the parameters, confirming the robustness of the method (Table 1).

The greenness and blueness of the proposed analytical method was evaluated using the Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI), Analytical Greenness Index (AGREE), and Blue Analytical Grade Index (BAGI) tools (Fig. 5 ). Initially, the use of organic solvents was avoided as they could have adverse environmental and health impacts. Hence, the sample preparation involved only aqueous media, thereby enhancing the greenness of the method. In addition, the use of the non-toxic and biocompatible GQDs as the sensing material further improves the environmental friendliness of the method. Spectro-fluorometric detection is a highly sensitive technique that requires minimal sample volumes, thus reducing the consumption of chemicals and reagents. Besides, the energy consumption for this technique is low compared to other analytical techniques like chromatographic methods.

The environmental impact of the analytical method was evaluated using the GAPI tool, which considers factors like the nature and quantity of chemicals used, waste generated, instrumentation, and energy consumption. The analysis yielded five pictograms, with 7 green zones and 5 yellow zones, indicating that the method is green and environmentally friendly (Fig. 5A). Additionally, a more comprehensive assessment of the method's greenness was conducted using the AGREE scoring system, which is based on the 12 Principles of Green Chemistry. The developed method scored 0.78 out of 1, designating it as a highly green analytical approach (Fig. 5B).

Furthermore, the blueness of the method was assessed using BAGI tool, which considers the analytical practicability and economic aspects of the method. Interestingly, a BAGI score of 72.5 out of 100 was obtained (Fig. 5C), indicating a high blue grade, suggesting that the proposed method is not only environmentally friendly but also economically viable and suitable for routine analysis of lumateperone in pharmaceutical formulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method to pharmaceutical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The validated method was successfully applied for the determination of lumateperone in its commercial pharmaceutical formulation. The drug content was found to be 99.79 ± 1.694 % of the labeled claim, which is within the acceptable limits (Table 3 ) without any special sample pretreatments. The results obtained from the proposed method were in good agreement with those obtained from a reported HPLC method, confirming the reliability and applicability of the developed method for the analysis of lumateperone in pharmaceutical dosage forms. Moreover, statistical comparison of the results from the two methods indicated no significant difference (Table 3), further validating the suitability of the proposed GQDs-based fluorescence quenching method for routine quality control analysis of lumateperone in pharmaceutical preparations.

In the present work, a novel, sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching-based method was developed and validated for the determination of the antipsychotic drug lumateperone using GQDs as fluorescent probes. Functionalization of the GQDs with tetraphenylborate ions enhanced the selectivity towards lumateperone, while the static quenching mechanism provided a linear response over a wide concentration range. Different experimental parameters affecting the fluorescence quenching process were optimized including pH, GQDs concentration and incubation time. The developed method was validated as per ICH guidelines, and the results demonstrated its reliability, accuracy, and precision for the quantification of lumateperone in pharmaceutical formulations. Additionally, the greenness and blueness assessment of the method using AGREE, GAPI and BAGI tools revealed its eco-friendly and economically viable nature, making it a promising alternative to traditional chromatographic techniques for routine analysis. Future research should focus on exploring the potential of other types of quantum dots for lumateperone sensing, as well as expanding the applicability of the developed method to other pharmaceutical analytes. Another area of interest could be the investigation of the sensing mechanism at the molecular level using computational studies. In addition, exploring the potential of the developed probe for real-time monitoring of lumateperone in biological samples and pharmacokinetic studies could be an interesting future direction. Overall, this work highlights the utility of GQDs as versatile fluorescent probes for the sensitive and selective determination of pharmaceutically important analytes, paving the way for their broader applications in the field of pharmaceutical analysis.
This research was funded by Taif University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia, for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-82).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New and emerging treatments for schizophrenia: a narrative review of their pharmacology, efficacy and side effect profile relative to established antipsychotics
Neurosci. Biobehav. Rev.

Howes, New and emerging treatments for schizophrenia: a narrative review of their pharmacology, efficacy and side effect profile relative to established antipsychotics, Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 132 (2022) 324-361.

Psychopharmacol. Bull.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Illuminating hope for mental health: a drug review on lumateperone
Cureus

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Tarzian, M. Ndrio, B. Chique, J. Serai, B. Thalackal, J. Lau, A.O. Fakoya, Illuminating hope for mental health: a drug review on lumateperone, Cureus, 15 (2023).

JAMA Psychiat.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.U. Correll, R.E. Davis, M. Weingart, J. Saillard, C. O’Gorman, J.M. Kane, J.A. Lieberman, C.A. Tamminga, S. Mates, K.E. Vanover, Efficacy and safety of lumateperone for treatment of schizophrenia: a randomized clinical trial, JAMA psychiatry, 77 (2020) 349-358.

Eur. Neuropsychopharmacol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metabolic profiling of lumateperone in vitro and in vivo by UPLC-Q Exactive Orbitrap HRMS, and its pharmacokinetic study in rat plasma by LC-MS/MS
J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Qiu, J. Guo, J. Chen, W. Zhang, W. Wang, Metabolic profiling of lumateperone in vitro and in vivo by UPLC-Q Exactive Orbitrap HRMS, and its pharmacokinetic study in rat plasma by LC-MS/MS, Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis, 246 (2024) 116221.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and validation of Uv spectrophotometric and Rp-Hplc method for estimation of lumateperone in solid dosage form
